Explanation: The report addresses chromatographic signal validation with structured methodology (preprocessing, ALS correction, statistical validation). Clarity is adequate; concepts like baseline drift and SNR are well-explained, though technical jargon may challenge non-specialists. Writing quality is good with proper formatting and appropriate citations (USP ¡621¿). However, critical limitations reduce depth and rigor: (1) massive data loss (11.5% and 99.9% missing values) severely constrains statistical power and introduces acknowledged selection bias; (2) the baseline proxy assumption lacks validation; (3) results are primarily numerical statistics without visual charts or graphs supporting interpretation; (4) no actual figures are included despite appendix references; (5) code appears functional but analysis execution completeness is uncertain. The approach is methodologically sound but not novel—ALS baseline correction is standard practice. The study demonstrates awareness of limitations but limited evidence addressing them substantively. The absence of visualizations (promised in Appendix A.1) significantly weakens communication of complex signal patterns and validation results.
